name: CNH Industrial N.V.

profile.description: CNH Industrial N.V. manufactures and sells agricultural and construction equipment worldwide through its Case IH, New Holland, STEYR, and Raven brands. The Agriculture segment produces tractors, harvesters, planters, and precision agriculture solutions. The Construction segment produces excavators, loaders, and graders. Financial Services provides equipment financing to dealers and customers. Founded in 1842 and headquartered in Basildon, UK, with $18.1B in 2025 revenue.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): CNH has incrementally advanced its precision agriculture platform through the 2021 Raven Industries acquisition and integration of autonomous technology, but has not executed a transformative business model pivot — the core tractor/harvester business remains unchanged [1]. The 2024 separation from Iveco Group was a structural simplification rather than innovation. AI Deployment (2/2): CNH has deployed AI across its product line with measurable customer impact — SenseApply sprayer automation uses machine vision to apply herbicide only where needed, reducing chemical use while increasing productivity [2]. AI Tech Assistant launched in early 2025 was deployed to hundreds of dealer groups to diagnose machinery issues and propose repair plans [2]. Every member of the global leadership team runs at least one generative or agentic AI pilot program [2]. Market Share (1/2): CNH holds meaningful market share in global agricultural equipment behind Deere & Company, with strong positions in tractors and combines under Case IH and New Holland brands, but is not the category leader [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): CNH unveiled a refreshed 2026 agricultural equipment lineup and targets making 90% of precision technologies in-house by 2030 (up from 80%), but specific product launches with revenue visibility are limited beyond general next-gen platform references [2]. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending relative to revenue is in line with agricultural equipment peers but does not stand out as top-quartile for the sector.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): CNH's purpose centers on 'breaking new ground' in agriculture and construction, connecting equipment manufacturing to enabling food production and infrastructure development globally. CEO Gerrit Marx has designated agriculture as his primary focus and articulated a vision of product leadership through constant innovation and putting customers first [2]. The purpose is clear and connects to societal value but is not uniquely differentiated from Deere or AGCO. Community Impact (1/2): CNH's 11 consecutive years on the Dow Jones Sustainability Index (World and Europe) with the highest score (88/100) among 126 machinery companies demonstrates sustained ESG commitment [3]. The company has embedded ESG targets in executive compensation and maintains environmental programs, though no specific charitable foundations or community investment dollar amounts were identified. Employee Retention (1/2): CNH's 34,197 employees operate under a recently simplified organizational structure following the Iveco separation. CEO Gerrit Marx was appointed July 2024, providing fresh leadership energy but limited tenure for cultural assessment. The matrix organizational model emphasizes innovation and quality standards [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): The Agnelli family's Exor N.V. maintains a significant controlling stake in CNH, representing long-term family alignment since the FIAT Industrial origins, though no specific recent open-market insider buying was identified.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on growth stock metrics. D/E of 333.7% exceeds the 300% floor (scores 0), reflecting CNH's captive Financial Services segment which carries equipment financing debt on its balance sheet — a structural feature of equipment OEMs with financing arms [1]. ROE of 4.8% is well below the 15% target, depressed by the agricultural downturn cycle. Revenue growth of -0.1% YoY and an estimated -8.4% 3-year CAGR reflect the global agricultural equipment demand decline that CNH expects will continue at -5% in 2026 [1]. Forward P/E of 13.9 and Forward PEG of 0.57 are both attractively valued, suggesting the market prices significant recovery potential. Profit margin of 2.1% is far below the 20% target, compressed by tariff headwinds (210-220bp agricultural margin drag, 600bp construction impact) and weaker volumes [1]. Adjusted EPS guidance of $0.35-$0.45 for 2026 implies continued profitability pressure.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): CNH's core business — manufacturing tractors, combines, excavators, and heavy construction machinery — involves complex physical-world production, global distribution networks, and dealer relationships that AI structurally cannot substitute [1]. Agricultural equipment operates in weather, soil, and crop conditions that require physical machines regardless of AI advancement. AI increases demand for CNH's products through precision agriculture adoption (SenseApply, autonomous driving), making AI an enabler rather than a threat. The captive financing segment involves regulated credit operations with switching costs. However, competitors like Deere invest more aggressively in autonomous farming, creating incremental competitive pressure from AI-differentiated rivals. AI Opportunity (1/1): CNH generates measurable AI-related value — the AI Tech Assistant deployed to hundreds of dealer groups, SenseApply machine vision sprayer automation, and real-time AI decision systems on combines and tractors represent genuine AI product revenue and customer value creation [2].

infinite_game.summary: CNH demonstrates mixed infinite game thinking. The company's 184-year heritage since 1842 and the Agnelli family's long-term stewardship through Exor reflect enduring institutional commitment. CEO Gerrit Marx's designation of agriculture as a core business to be overseen directly by the CEO — rather than pursuing diversification — shows focus on Just Cause. However, the recent leadership transitions (Marx is the second CEO since the Iveco separation), cyclical earnings pressures, and dividend cuts suggest finite game pressures competing with long-term vision.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): CNH's purpose of enabling farmers to feed the world and builders to construct infrastructure connects the company to essential human needs. CEO Gerrit Marx's decision to designate agriculture as his 'primary focus' and oversee it directly reflects genuine cause alignment over diversification [2]. The 184-year heritage since 1842 provides multi-generational validation. However, the purpose is functional (building equipment for farmers and builders) rather than transformative, and is shared by every agricultural equipment manufacturer.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): CNH's simplified matrix organizational structure post-Iveco separation is designed to drive innovation and ethical standards, with function heads empowered across the organization [2]. The 34,197-person workforce operates across global manufacturing sites with established labor relationships. However, CEO Gerrit Marx was appointed only in July 2024, creating leadership transition dynamics, and the cyclical downturn with earnings declining 94.4% creates organizational stress that can undermine trust [1].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CNH demonstrates an infinite competitive mindset, positioning itself to learn from Deere & Company's strengths in precision agriculture rather than simply attacking. The target to make 90% of precision technologies in-house by 2030 (up from 80%) reflects using rival capabilities as motivation for self-improvement [2]. CEO Marx frames competition as product leadership through innovation and customer focus rather than market share battles.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): CNH demonstrated meaningful strategic flexibility in separating from Iveco Group in 2024 to focus purely on agriculture and construction equipment — shedding the truck/bus/powertrain business to concentrate resources. The Raven Industries acquisition (2021) pivoted CNH into autonomous precision agriculture technology [2]. These represent significant strategic evolution, though not a full business model transformation.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): CNH cut its annual dividend from $0.47/share (2024) to $0.25 (2025) to $0.10 (2026), reflecting financial prudence over courageous reinvestment [1]. The company's 2026 guidance of flat-to-down-4% revenue with $0.35-$0.45 adjusted EPS shows defensive posturing rather than counter-cyclical investment. No evidence of bold long-term bets that sacrifice near-term earnings for future capability — the AI and precision agriculture investments, while real, are incremental rather than transformative.

investment_thesis: CNH Industrial is a global agricultural and construction equipment manufacturer trading at a deeply discounted 13.9x forward P/E and 0.57x forward PEG — reflecting severe cyclical earnings compression with profit margin at 2.1% and EPS declining 94.4% YoY [1]. Q1 2026 revenue of $3.83B beat expectations by 3.2%, with Agriculture net sales up 1% including positive currency translation. The AI precision agriculture strategy is compelling — SenseApply machine vision, AI Tech Assistant for dealers, and the target to produce 90% of precision technologies in-house by 2030 position CNH for margin expansion as the ag cycle recovers [2]. The Inspire Impact Score of 64 (ALIGNED) reflects CNH's strong ESG profile across 17 positive dimensions including environmental risk mitigation, product sustainability, and ethical supply chain management.
thesis_continued: The bear case is driven by the severe agricultural equipment downturn. CNH expects global ag equipment demand to decline a further 5% in 2026, with tariff rules creating 210-220bp agricultural margin drag and 600bp construction margin impact [1]. Adjusted EPS guidance of $0.35-$0.45 represents a 75%+ decline from peak earnings levels. D/E of 333.7% reflects the structural leverage of the captive Financial Services segment, but rising interest rates and Brazil credit costs add financial risk. The dividend has been cut from $0.47 (2024) to $0.10 (2026), signaling management's uncertainty about earnings recovery timing. Construction segment turned to an adjusted EBIT loss of $28M in Q1 2026 [1]. The Exor controlling shareholder structure limits minority shareholder influence over capital allocation decisions.
key_catalysts: Agricultural equipment cycle recovery beginning in 2027 as farm income stabilizes and pent-up demand from delayed equipment purchases drives replacement cycle — the 0.57x forward PEG implies significant upside if earnings normalize

Precision agriculture technology adoption driving ASP increases and margin expansion as AI-powered features like SenseApply and autonomous driving become standard across the Case IH and New Holland product lines
Resolution of tariff uncertainty reducing the 210-220bp agricultural margin headwind and 600bp construction impact, potentially restoring profitability closer to mid-cycle levels
key_risks: Global agricultural equipment demand decline extends beyond 2026 as farm incomes remain compressed from commodity price weakness, delaying the earnings recovery implied by the 13.9x forward P/E

Tariff escalation beyond current expectations — particularly US-China and US-EU trade friction — further compressing margins beyond the 210-220bp agricultural and 600bp construction headwinds already guided
Construction segment EBIT losses deepen if infrastructure spending disappoints, creating a cash drain that competes with investment in the higher-return agriculture precision technology strategy
sources: CNH Industrial Q1 2026 Earnings Release, 10-Q Filing, and Full-Year 2026 Guidance — SEC, 2026
CNH Industrial AI Innovation in Agriculture, CEO Leadership, and Precision Technology Strategy — Fortune, CNH Investor Relations, 2025-2026
CNH Industrial Sustainability and DJSI Recognition — cnh.com/sustainable-investing, Sustainalytics, accessed May 2026
